topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The afternoon light was doing nothing to lift the grey blanket that had settled over Leo. He slumped in his chair, the textbook lying open before him, a monument to unfinished work. Another day, another mountain of homework looming, and the familiar tightness in his chest made him want to just dissolve into the worn upholstery of his desk. He sighed, a long, drawn-out sound that seemed to carry the weight of every unanswered question. While rummaging through a forgotten box of old school papers in the attic—a task he’d been forced to do by his mother—he found it: a meticulously organized collection. Every assignment, every test paper, every doodle, perfectly preserved. A strange, hollow feeling settled in his stomach, a mix of bewilderment and a sudden, sharp sense of being profoundly misunderstood.

[story 2]

Maya stared at the stack of papers on the kitchen counter. They were crisp, the edges still sharp, a stark contrast to the usual chaotic mess of her desk. She picked up a crumpled history essay, the ink faded but the handwriting undeniably hers. A low, simmering frustration began to build behind her eyes. Why? Why would her mother hoard every piece of schoolwork? It felt like an accusation, a silent judgment on her inability to manage her own chaos. She traced a line of text with a fingertip, her jaw clenching so tightly her molars ached. The silence of the house amplified the internal churning; she felt heavy, burdened by an inexplicable sense of being watched and judged for her lackluster performance.

[story 3]

Ethan dragged himself to the living room, the effort costing him dearly. He didn't bother with the television; the noise felt abrasive. He was supposed to be studying for his upcoming math exam, but instead, he was staring at a large cardboard box labeled "Old Schoolstuff." Inside, nestled between faded report cards and crumpled permission slips, was an astonishing trove. Every single assignment from middle school through high school, cataloged with neat little tabs. A slow, heavy weariness washed over him. He didn't move to touch anything immediately. Instead, he just sat on the floor, his shoulders drooping, letting the sheer volume of organized effort wash over him with a profound sense of deflation. It was just… too much.
